summary: Modern CPUs have evolved far beyond simple arithmetic and logic operations, adding complex memory hierarchies with caches and TLBs, out-of-order execution, SIMD instructions, and sophisticated concurrency primitives. While many optimizations like branch prediction and cache management happen transparently, programmers can achieve significant performance gains by understanding memory access patterns, cache locality, and avoiding expensive syscalls. The gap between memory and CPU speed has grown to 100x+, making memory hierarchy understanding critical, though clever prefetching and caching often mask this latency automatically.
key_points: Memory access has become the primary bottleneck - memory can take 400+ cycles while CPU operations take a few cycles, but prefetching and caching can recover most of this performance loss
Context switches and syscalls are extremely expensive due to cache/TLB evictions, making batched syscalls and user-space I/O stacks necessary for high performance
SIMD instructions provide 2x-4x speedups for parallel operations, and modern compilers can often auto-vectorize simple code
Most 'old school' optimizations (branch prediction tricks, alignment padding, avoiding branches) are now largely irrelevant as CPUs have gotten much better at executing 'bad' code
Future trends point toward hardware acceleration (FPGAs/ASICs), user-mode I/O stacks for low-latency networking/storage, and dark silicon enabling specialized on-chip accelerators
money_quotes: A few cycles vs. 400+ cycles sounds really bad; that's well over 100x slower. But if I write a dumb loop that reads and operates on a large block of 64-bit values, the CPU is smart enough to prefetch the correct data before I need it, which lets me process at about 22 GB/s on my 3GHz processor.

The real cost of not locking also often ends up being the inevitable bugs. Doing clever things with memory barriers is almost always a bug waiting to happen... The fact is, any time anybody makes up a new locking mechanism, THEY ALWAYS GET IT WRONG.
14,000 cycles after a syscall, code is still not quite running at full speed.

CPUs have gotten a lot better at executing poorly optimized code over the past decade, and compilers are getting better at optimizing code, which makes optimizing branches a poor use of time unless you're trying to squeeze out the absolute best possible performance.

It's important to note that x86 has one of the strongest memory models of any architecture you're likely to encounter nowadays. If you write code that just works without thinking it through and port it to architectures that have weaker guarantees (PPC, ARM, or Alpha), you'll almost certainly have bugs.
key_insight: Modern CPU performance is dominated by memory hierarchy effects rather than instruction execution speed, and while many optimizations happen transparently, understanding cache behavior and avoiding syscalls matters far more than traditional micro-optimizations like branch prediction or alignment that CPUs now handle well automatically.
